response:
Decided and recorded in `DECISION.md`.

**Decision: free-tier cap = 300 requests/day per key.**

**Why:** The 30-day export shows per-key daily averages tightly clustered around 94–106, never near the memo's own 250 reference point. 300 sits well above every observed day (so no real free user is affected today) but well below the 1,000/day the metering rig happens to support — which was sized before any usage data existed and shouldn't drive the policy just because it's free to ship. Landing near the observed average (150) was rejected because the CSV only gives daily aggregates, not per-key distribution — I can't see whether a single bursty legitimate user (e.g., one big batch job) would get caught by a tighter cap. 300 (~3x the average day) gives headroom against that unknown tail while still being low enough to actually bind for genuine high-volume users, which is the whole point of the cap as a conversion lever.

**What would make me change it:**
- Users clustering just under 300 without upgrading once Pro is live → lower toward 150.
- Support complaints about legitimate one-day burst usage getting blocked → raise it or add burst allowance.
- Once 60-90 days of real Pro-conversion data exist → retune against actual behavior, not this pre-launch snapshot.
- Rising infra capacity alone is never a reason to raise it — that's the sunk-cost trap this decision is deliberately avoiding.
